_meta.source: build_companies.py — aggregates per-company JSON files in data/companies/
_meta.notes: Each company entry is hand-curated from that company's OWN published material (annual report, ESG/sustainability report, asset location pages). data_quality='sourced' means at least 5 country claims with strong evidence. Frontend should fall back to the modeled commodity-overlap heuristic for companies not present in this file.

country_claims.evidence: ADM operates the largest US soybean origination network, with 220+ grain elevators and crush plants in the corn belt. Disclosed in ADM 10-K segment reporting (Ag Services & Oilseeds segment, North America).
ADM operates 35+ Brazilian soybean origination assets and crush plants (Mato Grosso, Paraná, Goiás). Brazil disclosed as a major South America origination country in ADM 10-K and ESG report.
ADM operates ports + crush at Rosario and other Argentine facilities; Argentine soy disclosed alongside Brazilian in 10-K South America segment.
ADM operates a Paraguayan soy origination + crushing footprint (Villeta plant).
ADM is one of the top 3 US corn buyers; corn elevators concentrated in IL, IN, IA, NE, MN per public asset list.
ADM disclosed in 10-K as a major Brazilian corn origination party, especially in Mato Grosso / second-crop (safrinha) corn.
ADM owns/operated 4 Ukrainian assets (Donetsk, Odesa, Mykolaiv, Illichivsk). 2022-2025 operating status disrupted by war; ADM continues some Ukrainian grain origination.
ADM is a top-3 US wheat handler with elevators across the Great Plains.
ADM acquired Toepfer International and operates in Australian wheat sourcing.
Same Ukrainian asset disclosure as for maize; ADM is one of multiple international traders with continued Ukrainian wheat origination.

ADM has historically been one of the top 3 cocoa buyers from CIV; partial sale to Olam in 2015 reduced exposure but ADM retains chocolate / ingredient cocoa sourcing through its Cocoa & Wild Flavors business.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages by country
Ukrainian asset operating status post-2022 not fully transparent in public filings
South America (Brazil + Argentina + Paraguay) reported as a region, not always split
ADM.meta_notes: ADM is publicly listed; the 10-K (Annual Report on Form 10-K, filed annually with the SEC) and the Corporate Sustainability Report disclose origination footprint at a regional, occasionally country, level. As with other traders, exact tonnage by country-of-origin is generally not disclosed.

Bunge.hq: Registered office: Geneva, Switzerland; operational HQ: Chesterfield, Missouri, USA

country_claims.evidence: Bunge is the first global commodity exporter to achieve 100% traceability and monitoring of its direct and indirect soy purchases in priority regions of the Brazilian Cerrado (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, Bahia, Mato Grosso); direct soy monitored since 2020, indirect added in October 2024.

Bunge operates nine oilseed crush facilities and six grain mills in the US Midwest; key soy crush plants disclosed in 10-K including Council Bluffs IA, Decatur IN and Morristown IN, drawing from the major US soy belt (IL, IA, IN, MO, OH, NE, MN, WI, SD, ND).

Bunge operates oilseed processing capacity in Argentina and has equity-method investments in Argentine biodiesel producers; Argentina disclosed as a core South American soy origination country in the FY2024 10-K.
Bunge holds a 33% interest in Complejo Agroindustrial Angostura S.A. (CAIASA), an oilseed processing JV with Louis Dreyfus and Aceitera General Deheza located in Paraguay.

Bunge identifies corn (alongside wheat) as one of its principal sourced grains; historically operated US corn milling at Danville IL, Worthington IN, Crete NE, Red Oak IA, Muleshoe TX and Atchison KS, though this North America corn-milling business was agreed for sale to Grain Craft in April 2025.
Bunge's Brazilian agribusiness includes corn origination through its grain elevator and port network; corn is part of Bunge's principal sourced grains disclosed in the 10-K.
Bunge operated a corn milling plant in Queretaro, Mexico as part of its North America corn milling business (now under agreement to be sold to Grain Craft, April 2025).

Bunge operates seven wheat mills in Brazil including Moinho Fluminense in Rio de Janeiro (>600,000 t/yr capacity), one of the four largest mills in Latin America; brands include Suprema, Soberana, Farina, Predileta, and Bem-fácil.
Bunge lists wheat as one of its principal sourced grains and operates grain milling facilities in the US; wheat origination is part of the Agribusiness segment disclosed in the 10-K.

Bunge Ukraine operates an industrial transshipping complex in Mykolaiv (Nikolayev) including an oilseed crushing facility and upgraded grain terminal; Bunge sources wheat and grain through this Black Sea infrastructure.

Bunge operates a soybean and rapeseed crush and soybean oil refining facility at the Port of Amsterdam; the Amsterdam + Brest facilities together have ~2 million tons of annual processing capacity per Bunge's own disclosure.
Bunge operates a soybean and rapeseed crush facility at the Port of Brest, France, part of the two-site European complex with ~2 million tons combined annual capacity.
Bunge's Mykolaiv complex is described by Bunge as the largest export crushing facility in Ukraine and the leading sunflower oil facility in the world's largest sunflower seed origin.
Bunge operates conventional biodiesel facilities in Brazil that process soybean oil, and runs major soybean crush facilities supplying the Brazilian edible-oil and biofuel markets.
Bunge's nine US oilseed crush facilities produce soybean oil for food, animal feed and biofuel markets including renewable diesel feedstock supply.

Bunge held a 50% interest in BP Bunge Bioenergia, a JV with bp operating 11 sugarcane/ethanol mills across Brazil's Southeast, North and Midwest; Bunge announced in June 2024 the sale of its 50% share to bp (closing Q4 2024). After this divestiture Bunge no longer has a direct Brazilian sugar/ethanol footprint.

Bunge produces, blends and distributes NPK fertilizers including phosphate-based and nitrogen products through its Argentine operations; Bunge built a phosphate production facility at the Ramallo industrial complex and formed the TFA fertilizer JV with Asociación de Cooperativas Argentinas.

Bunge holds a 30% equity stake in Agrofel Grãos e Insumos, a Brazilian agricultural inputs reseller (fertilizers and crop inputs) that complements its soybean origination business, disclosed in the FY2024 10-K.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages by country for any commodity — Bunge's 10-K reports financial segments regionally (Ag Services & Oilseeds, Refined & Specialty Oils, Milling, Sugar & Bioenergy) but not tonnage by country of origin.

Bunge-Viterra business combination closed July 2, 2025: this adds material grain handling and softseed (canola, wheat) origination assets in Canada, Australia, Europe and Argentina that are NOT yet reflected in the FY2024 10-K-sourced footprint here. Post-merger 10-K (FY2025) will materially expand the country list.

Bunge sold its 50% stake in BP Bunge Bioenergia (Brazilian sugar/ethanol JV) to bp with closing expected Q4 2024; sugar entry above reflects the divestiture but post-2024 Bunge sugar footprint is effectively zero.

Bunge sold its Mexican wheat milling business in Q3 2022 — Mexico no longer a wheat processing-origin country for Bunge; only corn milling remained, and that is also being sold (Apr 2025 agreement with Grain Craft).
Bunge exited its Morocco phosphate fertilizer JV in 2013 — Morocco no longer in scope.
Sunflower origination footprint outside Ukraine (e.g., Romania, where Bunge has consumer oil brands) is not clearly disclosed at the origination-volume level.
Bunge.meta_notes: Bunge is publicly listed on NYSE. The 10-K (FY2024, filed Feb 2025) and the corporate sustainability reporting describe origination, processing, and milling assets country-by-country. Bunge redomesticated from Bermuda to Switzerland on Oct 31, 2023. The Bunge-Viterra business combination closed July 2, 2025 — that adds substantial wheat / canola / grain handling assets in Canada, Australia, and additional Europe/Argentina capacity that are NOT yet reflected in the FY2024 10-K disclosed footprint. As with other traders, exact tonnage by country-of-origin is generally not disclosed.

country_claims.evidence: Cargill publishes a Brazilian soy traceability dashboard and committed to a Soy Moratorium covering the Amazon biome; the Cerrado is publicly named as a critical sourcing area.
Cargill operates major soybean crushing capacity in Argentina (Villa Gobernador Galvez complex) and publishes Argentine soy as part of its origin portfolio.
Cargill operates ~50 US grain elevators and is one of the largest US soy buyers; disclosed in 10-K of joint-venture partners (Carval, AGT Foods) and Cargill press materials.
Cargill publishes a publicly-searchable palm oil supplier list and mill list, mostly Indonesia + Malaysia. Cargill's NDPE policy specifically addresses Indonesian deforestation.
Same supplier-list disclosure; Cargill operates the Kuantan palm-oil refinery in Pahang.
Cargill is one of the largest cocoa buyers from Côte d'Ivoire; publishes Cocoa Promise progress reports with country-level farmer counts.
Cocoa Promise reports disclose Ghana farmer engagement numbers alongside CIV.
Cargill operates major US wheat-handling capacity through its grain elevator network; consistently identified as a top-3 US wheat exporter.
Cargill announced suspension of new investment in Russia in March 2022 but continued grain trading; exited Russian grain export business in July 2023 per public statement.
Cargill operates 4 major US beef-processing plants (High River AB Canada, Friona TX, Schuyler NE, Dodge City KS, Fort Morgan CO).
High River, Alberta plant is one of Canada's two largest beef processors.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages for any commodity
Maize / corn trading footprint not disclosed at country level despite being a top global commodity for Cargill
Cargill exited some markets (Russia, Venezuela) but the date-stamped state of current sourcing isn't always easy to verify
Cocoa volumes by country: Cargill's annual Cocoa Promise reports give farmer counts but not tonnage by country
Cargill.meta_notes: Cargill is privately held and does not publish country-level customs records. Country mentions below are drawn from Cargill's own ESG/sustainability reports + press releases. Volumes are not disclosed; share percentages are NULL by design.

JBS.ownership: public; dual-listed June 2025 (NYSE: JBS / B3: JBSS32 BDRs). JBS S.A. continues to trade on B3 as JBSS3. Pilgrim's Pride Corporation (NASDAQ: PPC) is a majority-owned subsidiary (JBS owns ~80% of PPC).
JBS.hq: Amstelveen, Netherlands (JBS N.V. registered HQ) / São Paulo, Brazil (JBS S.A. operational HQ)

country_claims.evidence: JBS S.A. operates the Friboi beef business in Brazil with the country's largest cattle processing footprint. JBS analyses more than 85,000 Brazilian cattle suppliers daily using satellite imagery, farm geo-referencing, and government data; ~15,000 supplier farms have been suspended for non-compliance. JBS has committed to 100% individual cattle traceability in the state of Pará by 2026 (announced at COP28) and operates four Green Offices in Pará (Marabá, Redenção, Santana do Araguaia, Tucumã). From 1 January 2026, JBS direct suppliers must join the Transparent Livestock Platform (PPT) and provide indirect-supplier data.

JBS USA Beef operates nine US beef processing facilities split into four fed-beef plants near major cattle production centres (Cactus TX, Grand Island NE, Greeley CO, Hyrum UT among them) and five regional plants. The Cactus, TX plant employs 3,700+ team members and pays $2.9B/year for livestock; Grand Island, NE processes 1.4M+ cattle per year and exports to 20 countries. A $150M expansion was announced for Cactus.

JBS Foods Canada operates a beef processing facility in Brooks, Alberta with 2,800+ team members, processing ~4,200 head/day and supporting 500+ local cattle producers, with annual livestock payments of CAD 1.6B. A ~$90M expansion is adding a patty processing line and fulfilment centre under Canada's Agri-Processing Investment Tax Credit. JBS Foods Canada also operates Mountain Creek Farms in Calgary and a Belleville, Ontario plant.

JBS Australia operates 11 strategically located production facilities and six feedlots from northern Queensland to Tasmania, with weekly processing capacity of 50,000+ cattle, 80,000+ small stock and 20,000+ hogs. The Dinmore beef plant near Ipswich, Qld is the largest beef facility in the southern hemisphere — 3,400 cattle/day, ~2,000 employees, ~10% of Australia's total beef processing capacity. JBS Australia's Northern Division has five processing plants and five feedlots in QLD/NSW; Beef City (Toowoomba) operates a bioenergy system.

JBS Australia operates four beef and lamb processing facilities and one feedlot across New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania and South Australia. JBS invested ~A$20M to restart the Cobram (Vic) lamb processing facility, citing increasing demand for Australian lamb, mutton and goat for domestic and international consumers. Brooklyn (Vic), Bordertown (SA), King Island (Tas) and Scone (NSW) are also Southern Division lamb/beef sites.

Pilgrim's Pride is one of the world's largest chicken producers. Pilgrim's operates protein processing and prepared-foods facilities across 14 US states and Puerto Rico. Approximately 60,000 employees globally across all Pilgrim's businesses. Pilgrim's US is the largest of four Pilgrim's business units (Pilgrim's US, Pilgrim's Mexico, Pilgrim's Moy Park, Pilgrim's UK).

Pilgrim's Mexico operates as a major chicken producer through subsidiaries including Avícola Pilgrim's Pride de Mexico, FAMPAT, Gallina Pesada, Inmobiliaria Avicola Pilgrim's Pride, Pilgrim's Pride S. de R.L., Plan Pro Restaurantes, and Servicios Administrativos Pilgrim's Pride per Pilgrim's Pride FY2024 10-K Exhibit 21 subsidiary list.

Seara, a JBS S.A. subsidiary, runs vertically-integrated chicken (and pork) operations in Brazil. The Seara segment includes chicken/pork processing, value-added products, and prepared foods including the Incrível plant-based brand. Seara is one of Brazil's largest poultry exporters.

Pilgrim's Europe (Pilgrim's Moy Park + Pilgrim's UK + Pilgrim's Food Masters) operates 40+ facilities across the UK, Ireland, France and the Netherlands with 17,000+ employees. Moy Park is a leading UK poultry and prepared-foods supplier; Pilgrim's UK is described as the UK's number one pig farmer and higher-welfare pork producer. Moy Park was acquired by Pilgrim's from JBS S.A. for $1.3B (£1.0B) in 2017.

Moy Park has poultry operations in the Republic of Ireland. Pilgrim's Pride 10-K Exhibit 21 lists Irish subsidiaries: Arkose Investments Unlimited Company, Consumer Foods Van Sales (Ireland) Ltd., Pilgrim's Food Masters Ireland Ltd., and Rye Valley Foods Ltd.

Pilgrim's Europe operations include facilities in France via Pilgrim's Food Masters (chilled and frozen ready meals). Pilgrim's groups 40+ European facilities across UK, Ireland, France and the Netherlands.
Pilgrim's Europe operates in the Netherlands via Pilgrim's Food Masters (chilled and frozen ready meals). JBS N.V., the listed parent, is also registered in Amstelveen, Netherlands.
Pilgrim's Pride operates a prepared-foods facility in Puerto Rico per Pilgrim's Sustainability Report 'About Our Company' chapter.

JBS USA Pork operates five US pork production facilities: Beardstown IL, Louisville KY, Marshalltown IA, Ottumwa IA and Worthington MN. Beardstown employs 1,900+ and pays 265+ hog producers >$810M/year. Worthington processes ~20,000 hogs/day. A national union contract covers Beardstown, Worthington, Marshalltown and Ottumwa. JBS USA also signed an agreement to acquire certain hog assets from TriOak Foods.
Seara operates vertically-integrated pork operations in Brazil alongside chicken — disclosed in JBS S.A.'s investor materials as a single Seara segment covering chicken, pork, prepared foods.

JBS Australia operates Australia's largest smallgoods manufacturer, Primo Foods (ham, bacon, salami, deli meats), and processes hogs at ~20,000/week as part of the broader 50,000 cattle / 80,000 small stock / 20,000 hogs weekly capacity. JBS Australia is also the parent of Rivalea, a major Australian pork producer (acquired 2022 via the Primo acquisition).
Pilgrim's UK self-describes as 'the UK's number one pig farmer and producer of higher welfare pork.' Pilgrim's Europe page enumerates Pilgrim's UK alongside Moy Park and Pilgrim's Food Masters.
Primo Foods (JBS Australia) is described by the JBS Foods Group as Australia's largest smallgoods manufacturer — ham, bacon, salami and deli meats sold to retail and foodservice across Australia.

JBS Australia oversees Premier Beehive in New Zealand, described as New Zealand's number one bacon and ham company. JBS Australia also owns a controlling stake in Scott Technology, an NZ-headquartered automation/robotics developer for meat processors.

Planterra Foods (JBS USA's plant-based subsidiary, Colorado-based) launched the OZO brand of pea-and-rice-protein products fermented by shiitake mushrooms — 22g protein per serving — into US retail and DTC e-commerce.
Seara's Incrível brand is JBS's Brazilian plant-based platform; included in JBS S.A. brand portfolio and Seara segment disclosures.
JBS acquired Vivera (Netherlands), described by JBS as 'the third largest producer of plant-based products in Europe,' to expand its global plant-based food platform.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume or tonnage percentages by country for any commodity — JBS discloses facility counts, head/day or week, supplier counts and payroll figures, not country-level revenue or tonnage shares.
JBS Leather (hides) global sourcing footprint not researched in this pass — JBS operates a separate Leather business across BRA / USA / Europe but country-level sourcing is not enumerated here.

JBS Friboi indirect cattle supplier monitoring: JBS commits all direct suppliers to onboard the Transparent Livestock Platform by 1 Jan 2026 and to provide indirect-supplier data; the actual % of indirect suppliers covered (vs direct) is still in transition and not stated as a single number in JBS's own materials.
Brazilian beef supply chain coverage by biome — JBS publishes Amazon-biome cattle monitoring (~85,000 suppliers analysed daily) but Cerrado/Pantanal coverage is less granularly disclosed.

USDA-regulated US-imported lean trimmings or Canadian/Mexican beef cross-flows between JBS plants are not disclosed in country-of-origin sourcing terms on the public site (they are inter-affiliate plant flows).

Argentine / Paraguayan / Uruguayan JBS beef operations: JBS historically had South American beef plants beyond Brazil (e.g., the former Swift Argentina, Paraguay plants). Their current operating status in JBS's 2024 disclosures was not freshly verified in this research pass and is therefore omitted to avoid stale claims.

Sustainability reports prior to FY2024 referenced (sustainability.jbsfoodsgroup.com) — the URL appears stable but specific page citations rely on the live nav state; a fresh fetch is recommended before publishing for production use.

Brief flagged a possible 'JBS Smithfield acquisition' — this is NOT supported by JBS's own materials. Smithfield Foods is a WH Group subsidiary. The Smithfield claim is excluded from this dataset; consider re-checking the brief if a different acquisition was intended (e.g., specific Smithfield plant divestitures sold to JBS — none verified here).

Pilgrim's Europe France/Netherlands operations: Pilgrim's Food Masters operates across UK/IRL/FR/NL but the site does not enumerate site-by-site facility counts per country in publicly-indexed pages used for this pass.

JBS NV (the new NYSE-listed parent, ticker JBS, listed June 2025) Form 20-F was filed in early 2026 covering FY2025 — full segment-by-country revenue breakdown is in that filing but was not parsed line-by-line in this research pass.
JBS.meta_notes: World's largest meat processor by revenue. Operates four core business platforms: (1) Beef — JBS Brasil (Friboi brand), JBS USA Beef, JBS Foods Canada, JBS Australia (incl. Primo Foods smallgoods, Premier Beehive NZ); (2) Poultry — Seara (Brazil), Pilgrim's Pride (USA / Mexico / Puerto Rico) and Pilgrim's Europe (Moy Park UK+IRL, Pilgrim's UK, Pilgrim's Food Masters UK/IRL/FR/NL); (3) Pork — JBS USA Pork (Swift brand), Seara (Brazil), JBS Australia (Primo), Pilgrim's UK; (4) Prepared / value-added foods + plant-based (Incrível by Seara in Brazil; OZO by Planterra in the US; Vivera in Europe). Country claims below are tagged to the JBS operating entity for each. Brazil cattle traceability disclosures are exceptional vs peers — JBS publishes the Transparent Livestock Platform (PPT) and a public socio-environmental compliance dashboard for direct cattle suppliers in the Amazon biome and has committed to 100% individual animal traceability in Pará by 2026. Share-of-volume percentages by country are not disclosed in JBS's own materials and are NULL by design. IMPORTANT — the user-task brief flagged a possible 'Smithfield acquisition' for verification; that claim does NOT appear in JBS's own materials. Smithfield Foods is owned by WH Group, not JBS. The Smithfield claim is therefore EXCLUDED from this dataset.

Louis Dreyfus.ownership: private (Louis-Dreyfus family via Akira Holding; Margarita Louis-Dreyfus controlling shareholder; minority stake held by Abu Dhabi Developmental Holding Co. — ADQ — since 2020)
Louis Dreyfus.hq: Rotterdam, Netherlands (registered HQ) / Geneva, Switzerland (operating HQ)

country_claims.evidence: LDC Ukraine self-describes as one of the country's three largest grains & oilseeds exporters, specialising in wheat, barley and maize. LDC operates a multi-commodity Odesa port terminal on the Black Sea (250,000 MT grain storage, 4-5 Mt/yr export turnover) and the Haisyn grains & oilseeds warehouse complex (57,000 MT) in Vinnytsia region.

LDC Russia page describes LDC as 'a leading grains exporter in Russia,' focused on wheat, barley and corn, with ~900,000 MT of inland grains storage and a 50 KMT Azov terminal. (Note: LDC, like other Western traders, has been under scrutiny over Russian operations; current disclosure status on country page reflects the most recent ldc.com text.)

In Argentina, LDC specialises in exporting surplus wheat, corn, sorghum and barley to global markets, with 20+ transport, 30+ processing and 70+ storage facilities. LDC has been operating in Argentina since 1897 and is the country's third-largest exporter.
LDC's North America Grains & Oilseeds Platform is anchored in the US; LDC operates origination, processing and logistics assets across the US for grains and oilseeds.

LDC is one of the largest soy traders in Brazil; the company achieved 100% soy traceability to farm level for direct suppliers in high-priority Cerrado municipalities under WBCSD Soft Commodities Forum methodology, expanding from 25 to 61 focus municipalities covering >70% of Cerrado land-conversion risk. LDC has integrated soy crushing and origination assets in Brazil and is building a greenfield fertiliser facility in Rondonópolis.

LDC operates two agro-industrial complexes in Santa Fe province — Timbúes (handles >4 Mt of oilseeds annually) and General Lagos (2.5 Mt/yr soybean crush, 600,000 t/yr biodiesel — the world's largest soybean-based biodiesel plant, plus 30,000 t lecithin and 60,000 t glycerin per year) — plus Bahía Blanca in Buenos Aires province.

LDC is building a fully integrated soy processing facility in Upper Sandusky, Ohio, and opened a soy liquid lecithin plant in Claypool, Indiana. LDC's Integrated Report 2024 states the Group advanced expansion of oilseeds processing capacity in the US during 2024.

LDC operates oilseeds crushing plants in Tianjin (North China), the Yangtze River area (East China), and the Pearl Delta Region (South China), and broke ground on a new ~3,500 t/day soy crushing facility in Zhangjiagang.

LDC has operated a canola processing complex in Yorkton, Saskatchewan since 2009; an additional crushing line announced will more than double the facility's annual crush capacity to >2 Mt. LDC also runs a PepsiCo regenerative-agriculture programme in Saskatchewan covering 25,000 acres / ~26,000 t canola.

LDC originates sugar mainly from Brazil, Thailand and Central America. In Brazil, LDC participates in industrial sugarcane activities, operating 13 sugar mills through Biosev. LDC is among the top 4 exporters of Brazilian sugar, supported by the Pederneiras intermodal terminal and the TEAG export terminal in Santos. (Biosev mills were upstreamed to LDC in 2021 with a planned Raizen merger — the LDC sugar platform retains its Brazilian origination footprint.)
LDC sugar platform names Thailand as a primary origin alongside Brazil and Central America; LDC established a Thailand representative office to support sugar and grains & oilseeds activities.

LDC is active in all major coffee-producing regions with offices in Brazil. Brazil is one of the seven origins covered by LDC's Stronger Coffee Initiative (alongside Colombia, Ethiopia, Indonesia, Mexico, Uganda, Vietnam) and one of the main origins for LDC's certified/verified coffee production (1.4M+ 60-kg bags in 2020).
LDC has a Vietnam coffee office; Vietnam is one of LDC's main robusta origins and is one of seven Stronger Coffee Initiative origins.

LDC has been active in Honduras since 2011 and is one of the country's top-5 coffee exporters, originating from 25,000+ producers and exporting ~97% of processed coffee. LDC operates a 45,000 t/yr coffee processing plant in Villanueva and inaugurated a new biomass-powered coffee drying plant in Lempira (Feb 2024).
LDC has a Colombia coffee office; Colombia is one of LDC's seven Stronger Coffee Initiative origins and one of the main certified/verified production origins.
LDC has an Indonesia coffee office; Indonesia is one of LDC's seven Stronger Coffee Initiative origins.
LDC has a Mexico coffee office; Mexico is one of LDC's seven Stronger Coffee Initiative origins and a main certified/verified production origin.
LDC has a Uganda coffee office; Uganda is one of LDC's seven Stronger Coffee Initiative origins.
LDC has a Kenya coffee office (one of nine offices in major coffee-producing areas per LDC coffee platform page).
LDC has an India coffee office (one of nine offices in major coffee-producing areas per LDC coffee platform page).
LDC has representation in Ethiopia per its coffee platform page; Ethiopia is one of seven Stronger Coffee Initiative origins.

LDC self-describes as a leading originator of US cotton. The Cotton Platform is headquartered in Cordova, Tennessee, with merchandising offices in Fresno, CA and Lubbock, TX, 24 warehouses across AR/AZ/CA/MS/MO/SC/TN/TX, and two container-loading facilities in West Texas. LDC is advancing regenerative-agriculture adoption in West Texas.
LDC self-describes as a leading originator of Brazilian cotton; LDC inaugurated a new cotton logistics center in Zarate-area / South Cone supply chain; Brazil is one of LDC's flagship cotton origins.
LDC self-describes as a leading originator of Australian cotton; Australia is listed alongside US and Brazil as a flagship cotton origin.

LDC's Cotton Platform page lists India as a key origination market: 'LDC leverages its local presence and knowledge of market dynamics to source significant volumes in key origination markets, such as India, Australia, West Africa, the US and Brazil.'
LDC's Cotton Platform page lists Pakistan among 'key producing regions' from which the company sources cotton.
LDC's Cotton Platform page lists China among 'key producing regions' from which the company sources cotton.

LDC originates rice from Thailand, Vietnam and China for distribution to Asia, Africa and the Middle East. In 2022 LDC more than doubled year-on-year rice volumes originated from Thailand. Thailand is one of LDC's key labor/human-rights engagement origins.
LDC originates rice from Vietnam alongside Thailand and China; Vietnam is one of LDC's key labor/human-rights engagement origins for rice.
In India, LDC originates and merchandises rice for local and international markets.
LDC's Rice Platform page names China as one of three Asian origination countries (alongside Thailand and Vietnam).

In northern Côte d'Ivoire, LDC and the Louis Dreyfus Foundation established a project to build a sustainable, inclusive and traceable rice supply chain linking 3,600+ smallholder farmers in Boundiali to processors and local markets.

LDC manages 25,000+ ha of citrus groves with 14M+ trees across 36 productive farms in Brazil, plus three state-of-the-art processing facilities and a citrus port terminal at Santos. LDC self-describes as one of the world's largest orange and lemon growers and juice suppliers.

LDC operates a citrus port terminal at Ghent, Belgium, capable of handling FCOJ and NFCOJ in bulk and drums — Belgium serves as LDC's European juice distribution hub rather than a primary growing origin.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages by country for any LDC commodity — LDC's Integrated Report 2024 discloses business-line performance, asset locations and traceability percentages, not country-tonnage shares.
Sugar Central America origins not named at country level on LDC pages (only 'Central America' as a region); Biosev Brazil mill list is not country-specific public detail beyond '13 mills' headline.

West Africa cotton origins are stated as a region ('West Africa') on the LDC Cotton Platform page; country-specific cotton operations in Cameroon, Burkina Faso, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, Zambia, etc. are not enumerated as a single sourcing list (Zambia is named only via a corporate-locations entry, not a sourcing claim).

Russia footprint: LDC has been criticised externally for continuing Russian grain trading after 2022; LDC's own Russia page (cited above) still describes the company as 'a leading grains exporter' but does not disclose recent volumes — current status as of 2025-26 is therefore uncertain from LDC's own materials.

Florida orange juice via Sucocítrico Cutrale partnership (mentioned in the user task brief) is NOT corroborated as a direct LDC operation on ldc.com search results — Cutrale is a separate Brazilian family-owned juice processor and any partnership is not visible in LDC's own published material at the time of this research pass; claim was therefore omitted to avoid speculation.

Biosev Brazil sugarcane mills had a planned merger with Raizen (2021); the current legal status of LDC's direct equity in those mills versus Raizen's ownership is not freshly restated on the public LDC sugar page (2024 Integrated Report needed for definitive 2024-25 status).
Coffee tonnage by country: LDC reports certified/verified bag totals across all origins together (e.g., 1.4M+ 60-kg bags across six origins in 2020) rather than per-country tonnage.
Freight platform (LDC's seventh business line) excluded from this scope — not a sourcing commodity.
Dairy / fertilizers / animal feed / metals are emerging or adjacent businesses (e.g., the Rondonópolis fertilizer plant) excluded as not core sourcing commodities.
Louis Dreyfus.meta_notes: LDC is privately held and does not publish country-level customs records or tonnage shares. Country mentions below are drawn from LDC's own Integrated Report 2024, business-line pages on ldc.com, country pages, and LDC press releases. Volumes generally not disclosed by country; share_pct is NULL by design. LDC organises around seven business lines: Grains & Oilseeds, Sugar (including Biosev — though Biosev mills upstreamed to LDC in 2021 with a planned Raizen merger), Coffee, Cotton, Rice, Juice, and Freight. Country claims are tagged by business line.

Olam Group.ownership: public (SGX: VC2). Olam Food Ingredients ('ofi') is separately listed on SGX (SGX: VC2's sibling ticker OG7).

country_claims.evidence: ofi reports 100% traceability achieved across its direct cocoa supply chain in Côte d'Ivoire; CIV is ofi's largest cocoa-origin country and the flagship country for the Cocoa Compass programme.
ofi reports 100% traceability across its sustainable supply chain in Ghana; Ghana sits alongside CIV as ofi's top two cocoa origins under Cocoa Compass.

ofi names Cameroon as a Child Labour Monitoring & Remediation System (CLMRS) coverage country alongside Uganda, Brazil and Indonesia; ofi has published living-income reference values for Cameroon (with partners) in its Cocoa Compass reporting.
ofi cocoa direct supply chain covers Indonesia; CLMRS coverage includes Indonesia. Mondelez/ofi joint commercial-scale sustainable cocoa farm partnership for Asia is sited in Indonesia.
ofi lists Brazil among the nine countries covered by its end-to-end cocoa traceability system; CLMRS deployment includes Brazil.
ofi lists Nigeria among the nine cocoa-origin countries in its direct supply chain; ofi has published living-income reference values for Nigeria.
ofi cocoa Papua New Guinea operations disclosed on ofi locations page; CLMRS app was extended to PNG and living-income reference values published.

ofi is a leading supplier of Brazilian green coffee under its Central & South America coffee programme; ofi is also building a soluble (instant) coffee processing facility in Brazil to complement existing facilities in Spain and Vietnam.

ofi operates its own wet mills in Vietnam to process fresh cherries; ofi's Asian green coffee programme covers Vietnam, with 1,300 Vietnamese farmers trained on climate-smart practices under ofi Coffee LENS sustainability programme.

ofi self-describes as the largest exporter of green coffee in Indonesia; certification premiums of US$830,000 distributed amongst 14,000+ farmers in Aceh and North Sumatra. AtSource provides traceability into Indonesian origin.
ofi lists Colombia under its Central & South America green coffee origins; Colombia is one of ofi's 18 coffee-growing origin countries publicly disclosed.
ofi includes Ethiopia among its 18 coffee origin countries; ofi's Global Coffee Platform-recognised AtSource programme covers African origins including Ethiopia.
Olam Agri operates Queensland Cotton (acquired 2007) with seven ginning facilities across Queensland and New South Wales; Australia is one of Olam Agri's flagship large-grower cotton origins.

Olam Agri reports nearly 20 years of cotton operations in Brazil; one of the leading exporters of Brazilian raw cotton, with a Mato Grosso farming operation (since 2017) producing cotton in rotation with soy and corn.

Olam Agri announced (2023) a global regenerative-agriculture cotton programme initially covering the United States and Côte d'Ivoire, with Australia and Brazil to follow — confirming USA as a directly-sourced cotton origin.
Olam Agri's regenerative-agriculture cotton programme covers Côte d'Ivoire as a smallholder-origin country; cotton is a long-standing Olam West-Africa supply chain.
ofi processes California almonds at its Hughson Nut facility in Hughson, CA (acquired 2014); USA is ofi's primary almond origin.
ofi runs four fully-mechanised cashew processing facilities in Côte d'Ivoire and self-describes as the largest cashew processor in Africa, with over 3,000 direct jobs.
ofi runs fully mechanised cashew processing facilities in Vietnam; 48% of energy used in ofi's Vietnam cashew factories is renewable per ofi sustainability reporting.
ofi opened a A$60 million almond processing plant in Victoria, Australia; Australia is a secondary almond origin for ofi alongside California.
ofi sources hazelnuts from Turkey (plus neighbouring Georgia); Turkey is ofi's primary hazelnut origin.

Olam Agri operates an integrated rice mill on a 6,000-hectare irrigated paddy farm in Nasarawa State producing 36,000 mt/year of milled rice; additional processing in Ogun State and cleaning facilities near Abuja and Kano. The Value Chain Development Programme works with 22,500+ Nigerian rice farmers.
Olam Agri's Better Rice Better Life platform lists Thailand among origin sourcing countries; Thailand is a long-standing Olam Agri rice procurement origin.
Olam Agri sources rice through operations in India (alongside Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam) for distribution including its Ghanaian Mama Gold brand.
Olam Agri's rice supply chain includes Vietnam as an origin, supporting destination markets across West Africa.

Olam Agri acquired BUA Group's wheat milling & pasta manufacturing assets in Nigeria for US$275m, making it one of Nigeria's largest wheat millers; Nigeria is a flour-milling destination market for imported wheat.
Olam Agri runs African Flour Mills wheat milling operations in Ghana and commissioned Ghana's first state-of-the-art pasta factory.

evidence_gaps: No share-of-volume percentages disclosed for any commodity by country — Olam/ofi/Olam Agri publish farmer counts and traceability percentages, not country-tonnage shares.
Olam Agri's grains & oilseeds trading footprint (origin countries for wheat, corn, soy traded) is not granularly disclosed at country level on the public site post-SALIC transaction.
Wood products (Gabon/Republic of Congo concessions) excluded from this scope despite being a historically significant Olam business now under Olam Agri.
Edible oils, sugar, animal feed origin countries under Olam Agri not researched in this pass — Olam Agri publishes regional rather than country sourcing data.

Post-Apr 2026 SALIC majority ownership of Olam Agri means future Olam Agri country-level disclosures may shift to SALIC-aligned reporting; data captured here reflects the most recent Olam-period disclosures.
Coffee Africa origins beyond Ethiopia (Tanzania, Uganda, etc.) not separately verified in this pass — ofi lists 18 origins but country-level public detail varies.
Olam Group.meta_notes: IMPORTANT — 2022-2026 restructure. Olam Group's re-organisation plan split the original company into three operating units: (1) Olam Food Ingredients ('ofi') — cocoa, coffee, dairy, nuts, spices — which has its own management and is being separately listed; (2) Olam Agri — grains & oilseeds, wheat milling & pasta, rice, edible oils, animal feed & protein, cotton, wood products, rubber, sugar — now majority 80.01% owned by Saudi Agricultural & Livestock Investment Company (SALIC) following Tranche 1 closing in April 2026, with Olam Group retaining a 19.99% associated-company stake; (3) the Remaining Olam Group. Each country claim below is tagged with the operating entity (ofi vs Olam Agri vs Olam Group) so the entity-of-record is unambiguous. Olam's AtSource sustainability insights platform and OFIS (Olam Farmer Information System) feed traceability data primarily for ofi commodities (cocoa, coffee, nuts). Country tonnage shares are generally not disclosed in Olam's own materials; share_pct is therefore NULL across the board.

Tyson Foods.ownership: public (NYSE: TSN). Tyson family retains voting control via Class B super-voting shares (Tyson Limited Partnership).
commodities: Prepared / further-processed meats (bacon, sausage, lunchmeat, hot dogs, ready meals)
Cattle, soy, palm oil, pulp/paper/packaging (Forest Protection Standard scope)

country_claims.evidence: Tyson Fresh Meats operates six US beef plants: Amarillo TX, Dakota City NE, Finney County KS (Holcomb), Joslin IL, Lexington NE, and Pasco WA. Dakota City NE is one of the largest US beef plants — Tyson's own press release describes its 4,300-person workforce producing 'enough beef in one day to feed 18 million people.' A $47M expansion of the Lexington NE boxed-beef warehouse was announced. Tyson's FY2024 10-K (period ended Sep 28 2024) discloses sales of products produced in a country other than the US were less than 10% of consolidated sales, so the Beef segment is overwhelmingly US-origin.

Tyson Fresh Meats operates US pork plants in Storm Lake IA, Logansport IN, Council Bluffs IA, Madison NE, and additional Midwestern sites. Tyson's own 'Tyson Foods Major Locations' document and press releases enumerate Storm Lake (Iowa), Logansport (Indiana) and Madison (Nebraska — fresh pork + ham operations) as core pork facilities; a Council Bluffs IA case-ready beef+pork plant added 60 jobs in 2015. Per the FY2024 10-K, less than 10% of consolidated sales were produced outside the US — so the Pork segment is essentially 100% US.

Tyson operates a vertically-integrated US poultry production process (hatcheries, broiler farms, feed mills, processing plants) — headquartered in Springdale AR with ~23,000 employees in Arkansas alone, plus plants across GA, AL, KY (Albany + Robards), TN (a $425M new Tennessee complex announced in 2021), MS, TX, NC, VA. The Springdale Incubation Technology Center (75,000 sq ft) supplies chicks for NW Arkansas broiler and Cornish hen operations. The Pine Bluff AR plant (built 1991) produces fully cooked chicken.

Tyson de Mexico is described by Tyson Foods as 'a vertically-integrated poultry production company' with offices in Mexico City, distributing poultry, pork and beef to retail, foodservice and wholesale customers in Mexico. Tyson Foods' 'A Global Food Company' page on tysonfoods.com enumerates the Mexican subsidiary.

Tyson Poultry Thailand Ltd., established in 1991, is described by Tyson Foods as 'one of the country's largest fully integrated chicken processors and a leader in manufacturing fully cooked chicken products worldwide.' Tyson then expanded its Thai footprint in June 2019 by completing the $340M acquisition of BRF S.A.'s Thai and European operations — four additional Thailand processing plants (with feed mill, hatchery, breeder farms and contract grower base) producing fresh and frozen value-added raw and fully-cooked poultry for retail and foodservice customers across Asia and Europe. Brands include Grabits, Hot 'N' Kickin' Chicken, Speedy Pollo, and Sadia.

Tyson established its first Chinese factory in 2001. In November 2018, Tyson completed the $2.16B acquisition of Keystone Foods from Marfrig Global Foods — bringing eight plants and three innovation centers across China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand and Australia. In November 2020, Tyson Foods announced plans for additional production facilities for its International business, including a new plant in China expected to create more than 700 jobs.

The 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition press release explicitly lists Malaysia among the five countries (China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand, Australia) housing Keystone's eight plants and three innovation centers. Tyson's subsequent international expansion announcements (Nov 2020) reaffirm Malaysia as an operating market.

The 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition press release names South Korea among the five Asia-Pacific countries where the acquired plants and innovation centers were located (China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand, Australia).

Australia entered Tyson's footprint via the 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition. Tyson's 'A Global Food Company' page describes an Australian operation originally established 1997 in the Brisbane Valley as a purpose-built beef-patty manufacturer servicing major QSR customers in Australia. Tyson Australia is part of Tyson's Asia Pacific business.

Tyson Foods' June 2019 completion of the BRF S.A. acquisition included 'one processing facility in the Netherlands' alongside one in the UK and four in Thailand. In November 2020 Tyson announced plans to expand its Netherlands facility as part of an international growth push.

Tyson Foods' June 2019 BRF acquisition included 'one processing facility in the United Kingdom' producing further-processed chicken under Grabits, Hot 'N' Kickin' Chicken, Speedy Pollo, Sadia and customer-owned brands for European retail and foodservice customers.

country_claims.commodity: Prepared / further-processed meats (bacon, sausage, lunchmeat, hot dogs, ready meals)
country_claims.evidence: Tyson's Prepared Foods segment produces meals under Tyson, Jimmy Dean, Hillshire Farm, Ball Park, Wright, Aidells, ibp and State Fair brands following the 2014 Hillshire Brands acquisition. Tyson invested $180M to add seven production lines and 270,000 sq ft of capacity at Caseyville IL for Hillshire Farm and Jimmy Dean output, creating ~400 jobs. Tyson built a new state-of-the-art bacon plant in Bowling Green KY (production began late 2023, 450 jobs) for Jimmy Dean and Wright Brand bacon, plus additional Kentucky prepared-foods operations in Claryville. A new fully-cooked food production plant opened in Virginia in November 2023.

Tyson's sustainability disclosures describe the commodities and ingredients needed for day-to-day operations as 'wheat, corn, rice, soy, dairy and vegetables.' Tyson's Local Grain Services (LGS) program is described on Tyson's own ESG hub as a way to 'actively build resources for and relationships with local grain farmers who provide corn for [Tyson's] birds, to meet our commitment to use grain locally within our supply chain.' US Midwest corn + soy is the dominant feedstock for Tyson's US broiler operations.

country_claims.commodity: Cattle, soy, palm oil, pulp/paper/packaging (Forest Protection Standard scope)
country_claims.evidence: Tyson's November 2020 Forest Protection Standard targets four commodities — cattle/beef, soy, palm oil, and pulp/paper/packaging — globally. Tyson commissioned a Proforest deforestation-risk assessment which concluded 94% of Tyson's land footprint is no-to-low risk of deforestation, ~3% is medium-to-high risk, and ~3% has unidentified sourcing origin. Country-level mill or supplier lists for palm oil are not published by Tyson in the same granularity as peers, so country-level palm oil sourcing claims are NOT made here — the Forest Protection Standard is included only as a global commitment.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume or tonnage percentages by country for any commodity. Tyson's FY2024 10-K only tells us non-US-produced sales are <10% of consolidated sales — it does not break out country shares within the international tail.

Tyson does not publish a country-level palm-oil supplier or mill list comparable to Cargill's NDPE disclosure; the Forest Protection Standard names palm oil as in-scope but origin geographies (Indonesia/Malaysia) are not specifically claimed in Tyson's own materials and are therefore NOT enumerated here.

Tyson's soy sourcing is not disclosed at country level. The Local Grain Services program is described as US-corn-focused; whether Tyson sources any soy outside the US (e.g., from Brazil or Argentina) is not stated in Tyson's own published materials.

Keystone Foods sub-country plant counts: Tyson's 2018 acquisition press release confirms 'eight plants and three innovation centers' across China + S. Korea + Malaysia + Thailand + Australia but does not enumerate plants per country in a single public document. Per-country plant counts are therefore omitted to avoid stale or inferred claims.

South Korea and Malaysia footprint detail is light — Tyson's own pages confirm the countries are within the Keystone perimeter but do not describe plant locations, capacity, or product mix at country level.

Tyson Australia: Tyson's 'A Global Food Company' page references three Australian facilities and 1,500+ employees in Asia Pacific (not Australia alone) — facility-level details (Brisbane Valley beef-patty plant only) are sparse.

Brazil-origin chicken: the Sadia brand is now Tyson-owned for non-Brazil markets (acquired via BRF deal), but Tyson does NOT operate poultry production in Brazil itself — Sadia in Brazil remains a BRF brand. This dataset therefore makes no Brazil claim for Tyson.

Cattle traceability: unlike JBS Brazil, Tyson does not publish a public cattle-supplier traceability dashboard for its US fed-cattle supply chain; the Forest Protection Standard sets a deforestation-risk goal but supplier-level disclosures are not country-segmented.
Russia / European Union beyond NL/UK: Tyson does not list operations elsewhere in continental Europe in its own materials; the EU footprint is limited to the Netherlands plant from the BRF deal.

FY2024 10-K (filed Nov 2024, period ended Sep 28 2024) is the latest annual filing referenced here; FY2025 10-K (period ended ~late Sep 2025) would be Tyson's most recent annual SEC filing as of May 2026 but specific FY2025 segment-by-country revenue lines were not parsed in this research pass.
Tyson Foods.meta_notes: Tyson Foods is the largest US meat processor by revenue and operates four reportable segments: Beef, Pork, Chicken, and Prepared Foods, plus an International/Other category. In its FY2024 Form 10-K (period ended Sep 28 2024), Tyson disclosed that 'sales of products produced in a country other than the United States were less than 10% of consolidated sales' — i.e. ~90%+ of production is US-based. The international footprint flows mainly through (a) Tyson de Mexico (vertically-integrated chicken, offices in Mexico City), (b) Tyson Poultry Thailand Ltd. (est. 1991) plus four further plants acquired from BRF S.A. in June 2019, (c) China operations dating to 2001 and the 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition (Tyson Foods bought Keystone from Marfrig for $2.16B; that deal brought eight plants + three innovation centers across China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand and Australia), and (d) European plants in the Netherlands and United Kingdom acquired from BRF. US production is dominated by Tyson Fresh Meats (beef: Amarillo TX, Dakota City NE, Finney County KS, Joslin IL, Lexington NE, Pasco WA; pork: Storm Lake IA, Logansport IN, Council Bluffs IA, Madison NE, Columbus Junction IA, Waterloo IA, Perry IA), the Chicken segment (Springdale AR HQ + complexes across AR, GA, AL, KY, TN, MS, TX, NC, VA), and Prepared Foods (Hillshire Farm / Jimmy Dean / Ball Park / Wright / Aidells brands acquired via the 2014 Hillshire Brands deal; key sites include Caseyville IL and Bowling Green KY). Share-of-volume percentages by country are NOT published in Tyson's own materials and are NULL by design. Tyson's 2024 sustainability disclosures focus on a Forest Protection Standard for four priority commodities (cattle/beef, soy, palm oil, pulp/paper/packaging); a Proforest deforestation-risk assessment concluded 94% of Tyson's land footprint is no-to-low risk.

country_claims.evidence: Indonesia is Wilmar's largest palm oil sourcing country. Wilmar discloses that ~66% of its 234,334 ha of oil palm planted area is in Indonesia (Sumatra, West Kalimantan, Central Kalimantan), and Indonesian third-party mills dominate the publicly-published supplier mill list on Wilmar's Sustainability Dashboard.

Wilmar holds ~25% of its planted area in East Malaysia (Sabah/Sarawak) and operates 14 refineries with integrated crushing, seven refineries, one standalone kernel crushing facility and four bulking facilities across Malaysia and Indonesia. Malaysian mills are fully published on Wilmar's supply chain map.

Wilmar entered Ghana through the acquisition of Benso Oil Palm Plantations (BOPP), a company listed on the Ghana Stock Exchange. Wilmar's own mill list includes mills located in Ghana, and Ghana is named in Wilmar's Africa palm portfolio (alongside Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, Uganda).

Wilmar acquired approximately 30,000 ha of land in Cross River State, Nigeria, for oil palm plantation expansion (PZ Wilmar joint venture). Wilmar publishes a dedicated Nigeria sustainability brief covering its plantation, milling and refining operations in the country.

Côte d'Ivoire is one of four African countries (with Ghana, Nigeria, Uganda) where Wilmar holds oil palm planted area directly or through joint ventures and associates, per Wilmar's plantation business page.

Uganda is named in Wilmar's African palm portfolio (Kalangala / Bidco Uganda partnership). Listed alongside Indonesia, Malaysia, Ghana, Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire in Wilmar's plantation business overview.

Wilmar Sugar Australia (formerly Sucrogen, acquired from CSR in 2010) is Australia's largest raw sugar producer, crushing ~15 million MT of cane annually across 8 mills in Queensland to produce >2 million MT of raw sugar. Wilmar also holds 75% of Sugar Australia which operates refineries at Yarraville (Victoria) and Racecourse (Mackay, Queensland).

Wilmar holds ~62% of Shree Renuka Sugars Limited (SRSL), one of India's largest sugar refiners and ethanol producers. SRSL operates 8 sugar mills in India and two port-based refineries (Kandla on the west coast, Haldia on the east coast).
Through the Sugar Australia / NZ Sugar JV (75% Wilmar), Wilmar operates the Chelsea Sugar refinery in Auckland, New Zealand's only sugar refiner.

Wilmar's Chinese subsidiary Yihai Kerry Arawana (YKA, ~89.99% owned, listed on Shenzhen ChiNext) is one of China's largest oilseed crushers. Wilmar reports total soybean crush in China grew from 86.8 million MT in 2019 to 93.0 million MT in 2020, with continued expansion in oilseed crushing, flour, rice, oleochemicals and consumer products.

AWL Agri Business (formerly Adani Wilmar) is India's largest edible oil refiner and consumer-pack producer (Fortune brand) and a leading oilseed crusher. Wilmar increased its stake to a controlling ~57-66% in 2024-2025 making AWL a Wilmar subsidiary.
Wilmar operates joint-venture soybean crushing capacity in southern Vietnam and has disclosed plans for an additional plant in the north of Vietnam.
Wilmar operates the Yuzhny oil refinery in Ukraine (sunflower oil) — disclosed by Wilmar as the largest refinery in Ukraine. Merged operationally with NMGK in Russia.

Via the NMGK JV, Wilmar discloses crushing plants, oil refineries, margarine, mayonnaise and soap manufacturing in Russia; NMGK is the largest seller of table margarine, mayonnaise and sauces in Russia per Wilmar's own description.

Through Yihai Kerry Arawana, Wilmar is one of China's largest rice millers and flour millers. The Arawana brand has ~45% market share in Chinese consumer-pack edible oils and a substantial position in rice and flour.
AWL Agri Business (Wilmar India) markets rice, wheat flour, pulses, besan and soya value-added products under the Fortune brand in addition to edible oils.
Wilmar lists Vietnam among countries where it holds leading positions in consumer-pack edible oils, rice, flour and related staples.

Wilmar's specialty fats and oleochemicals capacity is concentrated in Indonesia and Malaysia, drawing on its integrated palm oil refining footprint. Disclosed via Wilmar's plantation + processing pages.
Malaysian refineries form a major part of Wilmar's specialty fats / oleochemicals value chain; integrated with the East Malaysian plantation base.
AWL Agri Business (Wilmar India) is described in Wilmar's India brief as the largest branded specialty fats and oleochemicals producer in India.

evidence_gaps: No tonnage by country-of-origin disclosed for any commodity; share_pct fields are NULL by design.
African palm origin volumes (Ghana, Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire, Uganda) are small relative to Indonesia/Malaysia but not split out individually in financial reporting.
Soybeans crushed in China are largely imported (predominantly from Brazil, USA, Argentina) but Wilmar does not publish an origin split for its Chinese crush.
Russia/Ukraine NMGK operations: Wilmar continues to disclose ownership but does not provide an updated 2023-2024 operational status given the war; this is a known transparency gap.
Sucrogen Australia's cane is sourced from third-party growers in Queensland; grower-level origin lists are not as exhaustively published as Wilmar's palm mill list.

Wilmar's Country Statistics traceability page lists more origin countries (e.g. PNG, Thailand, Honduras, Guatemala) that flow into its refineries via third-party traders; these were not included here at country-claim level because they are typically <1% of supply and not explicitly tied to a Wilmar-owned asset.
Wilmar.meta_notes: Wilmar is publicly listed on SGX and is widely cited as having the strongest supply-chain transparency among major palm oil traders. Wilmar publishes a public Sustainability Dashboard, a Supply Chain Map, a Country Statistics page, and downloadable own-mill and supplier mill lists (typically ~1,500+ third-party mills supplying its refineries in Indonesia + Malaysia, plus ~44 own mills across East Malaysia, Indonesia, Ghana and Nigeria). Volumes by country are not consistently disclosed; share-of-volume figures are therefore left NULL. Country claims below are sourced from Wilmar's Annual Report 2024, segment review documents, business pages on wilmar-international.com, and the public traceability dashboard.
